Hi, I'm Orin Klaff and you're here because you're interested in presenting, selling, pitching and so you're in the right place because I'm the author of Pitch Anything. If you go to pitch anything to anybody, I recommend you start by telling them your big idea. Here it is, the big idea in just 68 words. At the crucial moment when it's most important to be convincing 9 out of 10 times, we are not. It's very likely that when you pitch, you either give too much information, too little or the wrong kind and this is the attention problem. You solve the attention problem with the things you learn here. Did you know that any new idea you present to another person is initially filtered to the most primitive part of the brain? Right here. That part of the mind only thinks about three things. You present your great idea and the other person's mind responds like this. Should I eat it? Should I kill it? Should I mate with it? That's because the mind is wired to care first about survival and not your products, not your ideas and not your deal. This is a serious problem if you're trying to pitch anything. Okay, here's the deal. I'm not here to waste your time and I certainly hope that if I ever meet you, you won't waste mine. So I'm going to keep this short. When you become an expert at pitching, setting the frame, frame control, social dynamics, your sales go up. You do this stuff in the way I describe. You will become the best of the best. Okay, let me repeat that. You will become one of the best there is. It's a weird thing to hear in a way. It's a weird thing to say. I am the best. Now guess how long I've been doing this? 15 years. That makes me a senior citizen in my business investment banking. And lucky for me, I'm good at my job or I'd be out of one. I've closed $400 million and I'll close another $400 million without breathing hard. But you are the new blood. You're going to be in line for the big deals. You're going to go home with the coin. You're the future of selling. Now you look hungry to close deals and make sales and that's good. It means you're ready to leave behind all that stuff they teach about selling and learn what works in the real world. So now that you know what's possible, let me tell you what's required. You are required to spend time to learn some new stuff about how people really buy and how the mind works. And I want winners here, not pikers, right? A piker is someone who wants a push button method. Piker wants someone else to do the hard work for him. See, people learn how to pitch the right way so they can become great, grow businesses, close big deals, break records and make the dough. No other reason. That's it. You want a four hour work week? Go get a job as a lifeguard. The reason I'm here and I've been asked to talk to you now because I'm experienced with walking into meetings, getting on the phone and starting as the beta with no control, no status and no power. And then a few minutes I have people following my agenda carefully paying attention to me and wanting to buy what I have. I do this well not because I'm a genius or because I pitched hundreds of times. It's because I pitched hundreds of times in very difficult circumstances in front of banks and CEOs and Wall Street investment firms and I've learned the common mistakes. At this point in my career I have a better method than most people. No matter your situation, no matter your business at the end of the day, all of us are out to generate more revenue and increase our pitch to close ratio. And anytime you can get a better method, you move the revenue needle. What makes pitching and closing so damn hard, right? There's many things. For one, there's a gatekeeper of the mind we talked about. It's called the crocodile brain right here. The crock responds as I said before, should I eat it? Should I kill it? Or should I mate with it? Just to get started you need to know how to get your ideas past this gatekeeper of the mind in order to get a buyer's focus and attention. You, me, we all need some easy ways to deal with this. Otherwise, no matter how good our idea or product is, we won't sell it. If you make the common mistakes, your best ideas will bounce off the brain of the person you're pitching and back in your face. But I'm going to prep you so your next meeting with a client, customer, investor, or JV partner, your next pitch will go differently. So yeah, you'll be a trainee for a short while as you're learning this method. Think of it as foundation building, right? You got to build up the foundation before you can put a building on it. You know what I built on this foundation? A half billion dollars of business. Later, you're going to have a chance to think about whether this is for you. If you decide it isn't, nothing to be embarrassed about, it's not for everyone. But if you really want to be great at pitching a deal, then let's get started right now. It's not hard to learn. You just have to be into it for the right reasons. Becoming the best, cozy deals, making money. I have two great tools, a video and a PDF, and it will get you immersed in how to do this. You get the tools and as much as I can, I'll help you get really good at this stuff. So all pikers right now, leave the room and let's get started with the real guys, the real people. Now, we're going to start with the six pitching flaws. So now we're going to go deeper into firm control. Hey, Orrin, it's frame control. I know what the f**k it is. I invented it. The big problem is really the attention problem. In the attention problem, there are six pitching flaws. That's why we fail to get the right kind of attention from people. You're probably familiar with most of these. Super common problem. Too much information, too much detail, too fast. We've all done it. I'll give you an example of this happening. So a company comes to pitch me on a deal. I say, guys, go ahead, tell me what you have. This is what they tell me. We have a cloud-based social media, packet sniffing, B2B cross-platform that seamlessly integrates with mobile payment solutions to create actionable, big data. What are you talking about? I don't know. They say, Orrin, you're just not familiar with modern business models. I say, have you lost your f**king mind? WoW, Orrin, you can't say that. Hi. So we're going to go deeper into pitch anything, a nice and friendly version of pitch anything. Another of the common pitching flaws, and I know you're familiar with it, is being too vague. I'll give you another example. A company comes to me, I say, guys, what do you do? They say, we are that fastest growing company in environmentally friendly prefab manufactured housing, over 200 installs in our pipeline. I go, great, so you are a prefab housing manufacturer. They say, no, no, no, we connect manufacturers and retailers. So guys, so you are a B2B hub or marketplace for the prefab housing industry. They say, no, see, we connect the financial, stop. Guys, what are you pitching me right now? Can't be vague and fuzzy, you have to say exactly what you have. We can talk about the pitching flaws all day long, there's so many of them. No context. Pitch is one after the next, they just look like the same. Sometimes we can't tell the difference between one and the other. Is this the last company or the next company or the one we're looking at now? We don't know. You seem needy. Nobody likes somebody when they seem like they need your money. It's cloistering and unfriendly. Another pitching flaw is just going too damn slow. Sorry, I'll hold it down. Okay, but where does this all come from? That's what we've got to figure out. For a few minutes we've got to talk about this crocodile brain. Look at this face. If this looks like a biologist to you, you are mistaken. I know so little, but these are some things you have to learn if you want to understand how people work. Pitching is not the organization of information. The better you try to organize your information, it doesn't mean your pitch gets better. Pitching is about people. It's not what happens, it's who it happens to. Who involves the biology of the brain. So we've got to spend three minutes on this. Then we can move on to the fun stuff, getting in front of people and pitching a deal. We are hardwired to be bad at making presentations. It is caused by the way our brains have evolved. We don't have one brain, we have three. Our brain developed in three separate stages. First came the old brain, or crocodile brain, the crock brain for short. It's responsible for the initial filtering of all incoming messages. It generates most survival fight or flight responses, but when it comes to decision making, the crock brain's reasoning power is primitive. It simply doesn't have a lot of thinking capacity, and most of what it does have is devoted primarily to the things it takes to keep us alive. The midbrain, which came next, determines the meaning of things in social situations. And finally, the neocortex evolved with a problem-solving ability and is able to think about complex issues and produce answers using reason. Our thought process exactly matches our evolution. First survival, then social relationships, and finally problem-solving. As you can see from this, all human information, human-to-human information comes in through this crocodile brain. It's at the brainstem. Brain does not come to the smart, linguistic, capable, problem-solving, enjoyable, humorous, poetic, and appreciative brain. That is the neocortex. It comes in through the crocodile brain, which is concerned only about one thing, and that's survival. Attention, as you're starting to find out, is everything. That's why we care about this brain stuff. Seriously, do I have to be in my face? Here's where it all ties together. Who cares about the six pitching flaws, the 12, the 50 pitching flaws? There's probably a hundred. Who cares about the six? Because they tie to how the human brain will ignore you. If it's too vague, if it's too much detail, if it's too slow, if it's not visual, if these six flaws you create, the brain will ignore you. Remember, the human mind is concerned with survival. When it first hears you begin to talk, it's reaction, as we said before. Should I eat it? Should I kill it? Or worse, should I mate with it? Here's the thing. You give your information. If it's boring, the crocodile brain is going to ignore it. If there's red flags or it seems untrue, it's going to say, this isn't safe. It's going to run. If it's too much detail and it's complicated, the crocodile brain is going to summarize it and say, I can't deal with this. Mainly, you're going to get ignored. We have to understand information has to be packaged in a way that the crocodile brain won't ignore it. The brain cannot multitask. When it comes to paying attention, the notion of multitasking is a false one. The brain only focuses on concepts sequentially, one at a time. It can switch between different activities very, very quickly, but doing so is exhausting. Research shows that we are hardwired in such a way that we cannot multitask. Minor interruptions can double the amount of time it takes to finish a task or understand a point being made in a presentation. Once we get beyond that, we can really start getting into the social dynamics of human-to-human pitching. If you want to pitch well, these are the things you have to do. We're going to get past this quickly and talk about, again, person-to-person pitching. It's got to be fast. Not just talk fast, but you have to deliver the core concepts of your idea as quickly as possible. It's got to be novel. I can't feel, if you're pitching to me, like I've seen this one or two or 20 times before. Otherwise, I just start to ignore it. It has to be concrete. You have to say things that are real and connect to the real world. It can't be the abstract philosophy of what you're selling. Real value delivery is the real things and visual. This is interesting. The pipes into the brain that carry visual information are 50 to 70 times faster than the auditory pipes. If you want things to get into somebody's mind, faster. It's got to be visual. Most importantly, you have to give high-contrast options. This is totally different from what you've seen before. If you go into the CEO of Ford and you say, I want to give you some options, and those options are all shades of gray of each other, he's going to say, it's too damn confusing. You need to go into that guy and say, there's two options. One's $5 million takes two weeks. One's $30 million and it takes six months. Which one do you want? High-contrast. You're going to do things in these way, this way, this way, and then you're going to get in front of people and start pitching your deal. The crock brain is picky in a cognitive miser whose primary interest is survival. It doesn't like to do a lot of work and is high maintenance when it is forced to perform any work, such as listening to a presentation. It requires concrete evidence presented simply and black and white to make a decision. Now we're going to get serious about the work of pitching a deal and closing it. The reason you have to do the things we talked about previously perfectly is because now you're going to try and get social control, what I call frame control over the deal. I see this many times and maybe I did this when I was learning, you probably do this, you walk into a room and you say, oh, you guys are from Chicago, pretty cold there this time of year. They say, wow, it's not that bad because we get to go to the Bears games. You say, oh, what's wrong with Grossman's arm this year? My sister lives in Springfield and sometimes I get to go to the games myself. Oh, you go to the games, you come back. This is not pitching and this is not frame control and it's not anything. It's just geography trivia. If you do this yourself, you're not building rapport and you're not advancing the deal. Now we're going to talk about frame control. When two people come together into a meeting or a social interaction, they each bring a frame and that's just their point of view or their perspective. Frames come together. As you can imagine, you can't have two dominant frames in the same business meeting. One frame always crashes the other one, smashes it and absorbs it and that's known as frame control. So how do you know who has frame control in a given meeting or social interaction? If you are responding and reacting to the other person, they have frame control. When they stand up, you stop talking and are quiet and listen to them. When they go to make a text on their phone, you are distracted and you do something else. When they tell a dumb joke, you laugh. That means they have frame control. By contrast, if you tell a crappy joke and other people are laughing, then you have frame control and this is incredibly important to what we're going to talk about next, which is the power of frames. So the intrigue frame, we're going to get back into that because for me it's so special it lets you... Oh my God. Every ****** phone in this room, the benefit of getting frame control and understanding frames is that you solve the attention problem. Talked about earlier. When you have frame control, people are paying attention to you. That's what you want. It gives you the time to convey your ideas, tell your narrative, provide your information, tell your story, have your credibility accepted, make your deal or your product, your ideas concrete. You have to have frame control or people won't listen to you. You won't have their attention. Look, you don't have to rely on me to teach you about frames. You already know about them. You know about a time frame. So if you go to a meeting and you say, guys, this meeting right here is going to last 10 minutes. Go. That's a very different meeting from saying we have 60 minutes to discuss our ideas and have discourse and conversation about ideas. So a 60-minute meeting is exploratory and a 10-minute meeting is about getting results. So you already know how to control frames, the time frame. And the other one is the power frame. And I know you've experienced it. I experience it almost every day. I walk into a room. The other guys have all the money and they're the decision makers and they are controlling me. And they say, pitch us your deal and we'll tell you if we're going to give you any money or if we're going to sign a term sheet or give you a purchase order. They're using the power frame. We have the power. You have to understand how to recognize that before you can break it and then absorb them into your frame. So the moral authority frame, you know what that is. So sometimes the guy with the power frame will say, I want you to go out there and sell the customer this product. And you say, well, this is an older model and it doesn't work that good. And the guy with the power with the power frame says, it doesn't matter. We sell what we have. Go sell it. So if you want the moral authority frame at that point, you say, look, you can ask me to do anything. I'll dig ditches out back. I'll shine the trucks if necessary. Make sure they have the right amount of air in their tires. And I'll fix the copier when it breaks. The one thing you will not ask me to do is do the wrong thing and sell customers something that's not valuable. I won't do the wrong thing. So that's the moral authority frame because there's nowhere higher to go. You can't appeal beyond that. The power frame can't say, well, I need to ask you to do the wrong thing today. You go to jail for trying to break the morality frame. The moral authority frame cannot be broken if you use it properly. So you want to see frames come together. You want to know how to break the power frame. The guy who has all the money and the control and the boss, you use it with the moral authority frame. I know there's another one that you see all the time, which is the analyst frame. You present your product. So we have a squirrel nut scratcher. And it's electronic and automated. And it has a semiconductor in it. And the analyst pops up and he says, prove to me that it can last more than five years in the field. How do I know that it can take nine vaults and DC power? And we need to see a demonstration of the oscillation method that you use to manufacture it. Those kind of questions come from an analyst. And if you chase each of those conversation threads, what awaits you? Doom, won't get your deal done. So you have to know how to break the analyst frame. And certainly, what we're going to talk about later is the intrigue frame. So when you understand how to recognize frames and how to use them, you're going to capture people's attention. And when they try and get your attention, or they try and break up your presentation into pieces, then you can use your own frames to get control of the social situation. And this is going to be the cornerstone of how you're going to get control of a pitch and make people decide in your favor and to buy your stuff and do your deal and be influenced by your ideas. There are literally dozens of frames that you need to know about and we're going to get there. And they're just so amazing. So like there's the money frame, which I want to talk about for a second. So when somebody is really fighting you about that, you say, hey, look, I have all the money in the world. I don't need this deal. I don't need your order. I don't need the money. For me, it has to be fun. So if you're going to be grinding, complaining, and wanting things for free, you don't really care about the deal anymore, but it's not fun. So you and I have to make this fun together before we can do a deal. So there you can use the money frame. And you may be thinking to yourself, as I did one point, I've got to memorize three, four, 10 frames to be able to use them. It's much easier than that. Just write them down. An index card. This is what I do even today. I've been doing it for years. The card's down. And I hear this. You have priced this company at $200 million, and we don't like the price. And I say, oh my gosh, I have no answer for this. What frame could work? Oh, the analyst frame. Okay, I'd come back to you with my own power frame. Hey, guys. You can either have a high price and safety, or you can have a low price and risk. You can't have a low price and no risk. All right? So I can use my own frame. Yeah. No, it's not. No, you got it wrong. Frames. Once you understand how to recognize frames, you don't have to memorize what your frame will be. You look at your car and you say, oh, that's a power frame. I'm going to use the intrigue frame. That's an analyst frame. I'm going to use the time frame. That's a moral, the moral authority frame coming at me. Uh-oh, I'm in trouble. Remember the moral authority frame is the highest and best one you can ever use if you use it right. So remember, yes, I use this for deal making, but when I wrote this stuff down first and I gave my agent the manuscript, she goes, please, can I show this to my sister? She's going in for a job interview and she needs to understand frames and frame control and how the crocodile brain works. And when I turned the manuscript into McGraw Hill, they said, please, before this is published, can we show it to the salespeople? Right? They're going to use this stuff. So this is not for deal making. That's where it came from. This is for every day, whatever it is you do. Very powerful. It's the most important thing you can learn here. So there are literally dozens of frames that you need to know and you can be using every day. There's the fun frame. This is a great. So if somebody you know is grinding you on price, making the deal difficult and wanting more than they should get, you say, stop. Look, I don't need this order and I don't need the money. So this has to be, what else could I want? Fun. This has to be fun. So if you can't make this fun for me, I'm going to take my toys and go play in a different sandbox. So there's the fun frame and it's really effective in the right places. But the good thing is you don't have to memorize two, three, five, ten frames. Do what I do. I just write them down. I lay them out. Here we go. Let's start the meeting. Oh, you're going to use the power frame. I think I will use the time frame to counteract that. Oh, I see. Here comes the analyst frame. I use a power frame of myself. Once you learn to recognize these things, you can have them right in front of you. Oh, here comes the moral authority frame. I'll just use the, uh-oh, there's nothing better than the moral authority frame. So when you can get it, it's the best. So remember, this stuff is not just for closing deals and all seriousness. You can use this in whatever business you're in today. Yes, I learned it in conference rooms and in millions of dollars of deals, but this is fun stuff that you're going to use in your own business. When I turned the manuscript in from the book to my agent and she first saw it, she said, please, can I share this with my sister? She's going in for a job interview today and she needs to know about the crocodile brain and she needs to know how to deal with the power frame. So you can use this stuff in whatever you're doing and my job is to make sure that you can. Here's what's great about where we are. So we know the six pitching flaws. We know how to get into the past the crocodile brain and into the mind of the other person and we know how to get frame control, right? Now, all of this can be done in a very regimented, clear way. It's a process because I'm a geek. I might seem kind of cool right now and know this stuff really well, but I was not a natural and so I had to learn this stuff and I, as much as you need, step one, step two, step three. If it gets more complicated than that, I can't do it. So I had to break this stuff down easy. The great thing is you're going to go in. You're going to step one, pitch without raising any red flags, get past the crocodile brain. Two, you're going to get frame control. Number three, when you've done that, you're going to apply counter pressure. You're going to apply counter pressure. You could do this. So you can say at that point that you've done everything else correctly and you feel like you have the most momentum and the highest status and the most amount of attention focused on you and you feel like a celebrity at that moment. You're going to say, so that's what I have and you're going to see why we are so busy and there's so much demand for what we have. And you know, we're choosing our customers at this point and we're only working with people who are fun and cool and nice to work with. And what I need you to do is tell me about yourself. Why are you a good customer? Why should I choose you as the customer I'm going to work with when I can work with anybody? That's the counter pressure you're going to apply. And remember, you're never, I can't do it. You're not going to walk in a room and say, okay, why should I choose you as a customer? It's not going to work. They're going to call security. But if you pitch well, you get frame control, you raise your status, you do it in this way and you feel the momentum, then you're going to, then you can apply counter pressure. I'm going to take my toys and go play in a different sandbox. If you're not a cool guy that I would like to have as a customer. And that's when you can apply counter pressure and you've given a great pitch in the pitch anything way. So there's very, very specific way to do this. If your pitch is 20 minutes, there's something to do in every single minute. I'm going to show you right now, minute by minute, the exact things you're going to do and say. Hey, Orin, time's up man. We got a wrap. Oh, here comes the time frame I see. So you think you're going to pull that on me? No, this isn't the time frame. This is the electricity frame. Oh, what's going on there guys? All right, turn that light back on and everyone on this crew, me, Mountain the Park and Lot in two minutes. Is this rolling? So there I am. I'm flying to Russia, right? Going to Moscow to close the world's easiest deal. Had it all lined up ready to go and I'd land to take a cab to my meeting and I'm sitting down with an oligarch, a Russian billionaire and he turns to me and he says, give me one last reason why I would do this deal. I said, Ivan, you know, this reminds me of the blue whale. You know the blue whale? This giant, incredible, majestic creature weighs many tons, swims the seven seas and has no natural enemies. No predator can push the blue whale around. This amazing creature will eat a thousand pounds of plankton twice a day. This incredible creature does whatever it wants whenever it wants. Yet as strong and incredible as it is, it will choke on the common cheese sandwich. You know why that is? He says, Aran, why is this? Ivan, because it just f**king is and that's why you're going to do this deal. So press hard. That copy's yours.
